title: Spencerian, oval and shaded downstroke
subtitle: The two foundational moves of the American business hand
excerpt: Spencerian script uses a very flexible pointed nib at a 52° slope. The hand is lighter and more airy than Copperplate: shades are applied only on specific downstrokes, the main oval and the descending stem, and hairlines are used everywhere else. This tutorial establishes the Spencerian oval and the shaded downstroke.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, The Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), and the Spencer Brothers' instructional copybooks, all public domain. Internet Archive: archive.org/details/spenceriankeytop00spen. The Spencerian system was codified by the Spencer family from the 1850s and was the dominant business script in the United States for several decades.

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrow, elongated oval that is the model letter form in Spencerian script. Unlike the Copperplate oval, the Spencerian oval is written with a shade on only one side, the left downstroke, and a hairline on all other sides.
The thick downstroke produced by applying pressure on the flexible nib so that the tines spread.
The thinnest possible line, produced with minimal nib pressure so the tines barely separate.

content.text: Spencerian is the pointed-nib hand that preceded and influenced Copperplate as a business writing system in the United States. It differs from Copperplate in two fundamental ways: the letters are more airy, with less shade coverage; and the
, the model form, is narrower and more vertical than the Copperplate oval. The
appears only on the principal downstroke; all other strokes are
. The technique builds on Copperplate stroke fundamentals.

items.name: Hunt 101 nib (highly flexible, the traditional Spencerian practice nib)
Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, with guide lines at 52° slope and 3 mm x-height

content.text: Begin at the top of the oval, slightly to the right of centre. The starting position is closer to the 12-o'clock position than in Copperplate. Draw a hairline curve upward and over to the left, then downward into the left side of the oval.

At the leftmost point of the oval, apply pressure. The tines spread as the stroke continues downward to the baseline. The shade occupies only this left-and-down section of the oval, approximately one quarter of the full oval path.
At the baseline, release all pressure. Continue with a hairline curve upward to the right, returning to the start position. The closed oval consists of one shaded section and three hairline sections.

The shaded downstroke (for letter stems): begin at the x-height with minimal pressure, apply shade pressure on the downstroke, then release at the baseline and continue with a hairline. The transition must be precise, in Spencerian the shade occupies only the downstroke, never the curve at the foot of the letter.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in Spencerian oval and shade practice.

items.symptom: The oval looks like a full shade all the way around, too heavy
items.cause: Pressure is being applied throughout the oval rather than only on the principal downstroke.
items.fix: In Spencerian, the nib must lift almost to hairline pressure immediately after the shaded downstroke section. Treat the pressure release as a positive action, consciously ease the tines closed as the stroke curves up from the baseline.

items.symptom: The Hunt 101 nib spatters on every upstroke
items.cause: The Hunt 101 is very flexible and responds to tiny variations in surface texture. Any catch on a paper fibre at hairline pressure throws the nib tip off course.
items.fix: Dust the paper surface with gum sandarac before using the Hunt 101. Work on a smooth, sized surface. If spattering persists, switch to the Nikko G until surface and pressure control are more established.
